nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on steep outer reef slopes and drop-offs over coral and sand, typically at depths of 5–70 m though recorded as deep as 119 m; tends to forage in the water column above the reef rather than directly on the substrate.

diet.notes: Has been documented to feed primarily on zooplankton, with a particular preference for gelatinous forms, along with fish eggs and small crustaceans — atypical for surgeonfishes, which are mostly grazing herbivores. Multiple small feedings throughout the day are commonly advised. Even though this species is principally a planktivore, regular offerings of marine-based seaweed and algae (e.g., Nori, Spirulina) are generally recommended to support immune health and reduce HLLE risk.

summary: A small Indo-Pacific surgeonfish with a uniformly dark brown body and contrasting pale (often snow-white) caudal fin. Unusually for tangs, it feeds primarily on zooplankton rather than algae and is generally considered one of the more peaceful surgeonfishes for reef aquariums.
careNotes:
Thompson's Surgeonfish is generally considered one of the more peaceful surgeonfishes in the hobby, though it has been reported to show aggression toward other surgeonfishes and similarly shaped tankmates — keeping a single specimen per tank is widely advised. The species tends to be an active swimmer that uses the entire water column, so ample open swimming space combined with live rock for grazing and shelter is commonly recommended. Strong, well-aerated water movement is generally advised to mimic the steep outer reef habitat where this species naturally occurs.

Unusually for an Acanthurus tang, this species is primarily a zooplankton feeder rather than an algae grazer; it has been documented preferentially consuming gelatinous zooplankton, fish eggs, and small crustaceans in the wild. In captivity, frequent small meaty feedings (mysis, brine, finely chopped seafood) are commonly recommended, but regular offerings of Nori and Spirulina-based foods are still generally advised to support immune health and reduce susceptibility to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE), to which most tangs are prone.

Tank-size guidance varied notably across sources: LiveAquaria recommends a minimum of 250 US gallons (~946 L), Quality Marine suggests 125 gal or larger (~473 L), and AquaticCommunity gives a rough ~50 gal per adult fish (~190 L) figure. Given the species' active nature, full-water-column use, sensitivity to HLLE, and the conservative-larger bias appropriate for tang husbandry, ~700 L is offered here as a sensible target, but dataStatus is set to needs_review on account of the genuine spread between sources.

Like all Acanthurus surgeonfishes, this species possesses a sharp caudal-peduncle spine (the "scalpel") that can inflict painful wounds — handle with care during net transfer or tank maintenance. The Indian Ocean population is reported to lack the pale caudal fin characteristic of Pacific specimens, which can cause confusion at point of sale.

Temperature range was averaged across four sources (LiveAquaria 72–78°F = 22–26°C, FishBase 24–27°C, Quality Marine 72–78°F, AquaticCommunity ~21–28°C). Adult size ranges across sources clustered tightly at 25–28 cm (FishBase 27 cm; LiveAquaria 11 in = 28 cm; Quality Marine ~10 in = 25 cm; AquaticCommunity 27 cm). pH spanned 8.0–8.4 (LiveAquaria/Quality Marine 8.1–8.4; AquaticCommunity 8.0–8.4); the lower bound was set to 8.0 as a result. Reef-safe rating reflects an averaging of LiveAquaria ("yes") and Quality Marine ("may nibble small invertebrates if underfed") to "with caution."
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Acanthurus thompsoni has not been established. Wild reproduction is presumed to occur via broadcast spawning typical of Acanthurus species, but no published aquarium protocol is known.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary product page (collections root returned 503). Source of LiveAquaria-stated tank size (250 gal), water parameters, max size, peaceful temperament, and reef-safe rating.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Acanthuridae, order Acanthuriformes), native range (Indo-Pacific, East Africa to Hawaiian/Marquesan/Ducie), depth range (0–119 m typical 5–70 m), max size (27 cm TL), temperature range (24–27°C), and zooplanktivore diet (trophic level 3.6, gelatinous zooplankton + fish eggs + crustaceans).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Third source for cross-verification on tank-size disagreement (suggests 125 gal minimum vs LiveAquaria's 250 gal). Confirmed water parameters, ~10 in max size, conspecific aggression toward similar tangs, reef-safe with caution (may nibble invertebrates if underfed), and the caudal-scalpel handling warning.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Fourth source: rough tank guidance (~50 gal/adult), pH 8.0–8.4, temperature 70s–low 80s°F, incompatibility with groupers/lionfish, sensitivity to chemical/physical/social changes, and no documented captive breeding.

fish.breedingNotes: No documented captive-breeding protocol; wild reproduction presumed to be broadcast spawning typical of Acanthurus.
